coredata.dc:title: Mechanism of NP@CQDs in the detection of quinolone antibiotics in food waste digestate filtrate based on fluorescence sensitization effect

Zhang, Wei
Yang, Yifan

coredata.dc:description:
In this study, nitrogen-phosphorus co-doped quantum dots (NP@CQDs) were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method using citric acid and ammonium dihydrogen phosphate as raw materials. They were used for the detection of three quinolone antibiotics (ciprofloxacin, norfloxacin and ofloxacin) in food waste digestate filtrate with good accuracy and applicability. The NP@CQDs have good stability of fluorescence properties with a quantum yield of up to 43 %. Taking ofloxacin as an example, the linearity was good in the concentration range of 5μmol/L to 300μmol/L (R
2 =0.991), and the limit of detection was 0.035μmol/L. Experimentally, it was demonstrated that the electron-donating groups on the NP@CQDs could interact with the aromatic ring groups in the antibiotics in a substituent manner, the degree of conjugation was enhanced, the excited state electron transfer was restricted, the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) reaction was suppressed, and the fluorescence was enhanced. This study provides an opportunity to develop a fluorescent ratio type probe for the detection of trace quinolone antibiotics in complex water samples rich in interferants.

head.ce:title: Mechanism of NP@CQDs in the detection of quinolone antibiotics in food waste digestate filtrate based on fluorescence sensitization effect

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In this study, nitrogen-phosphorus co-doped quantum dots (NP@CQDs) were synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method using citric acid and ammonium dihydrogen phosphate as raw materials. They were used for the detection of three quinolone antibiotics (ciprofloxacin, norfloxacin and ofloxacin) in food waste digestate filtrate with good accuracy and applicability. The NP@CQDs have good stability of fluorescence properties with a quantum yield of up to 43 %. Taking ofloxacin as an example, the linearity was good in the concentration range of 5μmol/L to 300μmol/L (R
2 =0.991), and the limit of detection was 0.035μmol/L. Experimentally, it was demonstrated that the electron-donating groups on the NP@CQDs could interact with the aromatic ring groups in the antibiotics in a substituent manner, the degree of conjugation was enhanced, the excited state electron transfer was restricted, the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) reaction was suppressed, and the fluorescence was enhanced. This study provides an opportunity to develop a fluorescent ratio type probe for the detection of trace quinolone antibiotics in complex water samples rich in interferants.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotics have become a pressing challenge to human health in the 21st century[1]. About 30–90 percent of antibiotics consumed by livestock and humans are released into the environment through urine and manure, thus accelerating the spread of antibiotics[2]. Among them, food waste, as a major component of household waste[3], may also be a major source of antibiotics[4]. Because household waste is not segregated at the time of collection, food waste may be contaminated with other antibiotic-rich household waste[5]. Therefore, more attention should be paid to antibiotics in food waste in subsequent studies. Effective treatment of food waste has always been a major problem and challenge in solid waste treatment issues. Food waste is not suitable for being landfilled or incinerated due to its high moisture content, low pH and low C/N ratio[6]. Anaerobic digestion is considered to be an effective resource treatment method, which can not only effectively degrade the organic matter in food waste, but also recover energy substances such as biogas[7]. Therefore, it is widely used in the treatment of food waste. Digestate is the main by-product of anaerobic fermentation of food waste, and it also has a high moisture content, which will lead to secondary pollution if it is not properly treated. Therefore, digestate will be dewatered to reduce its water content before secondary utilization, which will produce a large amount of dewatered filtrate. The dewatered filtrate not only retains a large amount of rich organic matter and humic acid-like substances[8]，but is also rich in nutrients such as nitrogen and phosphorus[9]，which can be used as a raw material for the production of organic fertilizers, soil conditioners and manure.

But inevitably, trace antibiotics[10] contained in the filtrate can be potentially harmful to the ecosystem and human beings. Therefore, rapid and accurate monitoring of antibiotics from digestate filtrate is of great importance for the subsequent utilization of digestate filtrate. The difficulty of antibiotic detection is further exacerbated by the fact that antibiotics, due to their adsorption and desorption properties, bind to organic substances (e.g., extracellular polymers) in the digestate filtrate to form EPS-antibiotic complexes[11]. Not only that, dissolved organic matter (DOM) in digestate also interacts with antibiotics and affects their mobility[12]. Among the oxyquinoline antibiotics, the position of the aromatic ring in the ciprofloxacin, norfloxacin and ofloxacin is greatly affected by DOM[13], and the three have solid-water distribution coefficients (Kd) of as high as 1.19, 1.42, and 0.98 L/g[14]. Therefore, in environmental analyses of micropollutants involving complex environmental matrices (e.g. digestate, sewage sludge, etc.), especially in multi-class analyses, rigorous sample preparation is often required[15]. The sample preparation procedure is the cornerstone of the instrumental analytical process and usually involves three steps: extraction, purification and determination[16,17]. Extraction processes are often long and cumbersome, requiring expensive equipment, and sometimes they are not effective in improving the analytical performance of the methods[16]. The decontamination process usually needs to be carried out by using expensive solid phase extraction (SPE) [17] techniques. Subsequent detection steps are usually based on the coupling of liquid chromatography with mass spectrometry or tandem mass spectrometry[18]. Therefore, the development of a rapid, convenient, inexpensive and effective method for the detection of antibiotics in digestate filtrate is urgent.

Fluorescent probes have come into the public's view owing to their high sensitivity and simple and fast detection process [19]. Carbon dots (CDs)-based ratio metric fluorescent probes have received extensive attention due to their excellent biocompatibility, water solubility, and multi-emission capability [20]. Fluorescent probes based on carbon dots typically have three components[21]: a recognition group (action group), a linker group, and a fluorophore (signal group). The recognition group realizes selective interactions between the probe and the detection object, the linking group connects the recognition group and the fluorophore, and the signal group produces an observable fluorescence signal[22]. There have been applications in the detection of heavy metals and antibiotics. However, the low quantum yield (QY) and low stability of carbon quantum dots limit their applications[23]. Various methods have been developed to modulate carbon quantum dots (CQDs) fluorescence and physicochemical properties. These methods include surface passivation and heteroatomic doping. Nevertheless, the surface passivation technique is complicated, and the majority of the organic reagents or catalysts used are cytotoxic and environmentally harmful[24–26]. Heteroatom doping can provide more active sites on the surface of CQDs. As a result, it can increase the QY of the surface defective states[27], improve the stability and reactivity of CQDs, realize better antibiotic detection, and make up for the lack of antibiotic detection in complex environmental samples. In previous studies, atoms such as N, S and P were mostly used for doping, which not only effectively reduced the detection limit of antibiotic detection, but also successfully applied to the detection of antibiotics in actual water samples such as milk[28], serum[29] and tap water[30,31]. However, at present, there is a lack of specific detection of antibiotics in food waste digestate filtrate, and it is not possible to eliminate the interference from organic matter, heavy metals and other substances in food waste digestate filtrate during the detection of antibiotic content.

In this study, NP@CQDs were prepared by a one-step hydrothermal method using citric acid as raw material and ammonium dihydrogen phosphate as nitrogen and phosphate sources. In addition, the synthesized NP@CQDs were used for the detection of three quinolone antibiotics (CIP, NOR and OFLX), and the selectivity, immunity and sensitivity of NP@CQDs for the detection of antibiotics were analyzed. Simultaneously, the fluorescence enhancement mechanism during the detection process was discussed. Finally, the possibility of using NP@CQDs as fluorescent probes for the detection of antibiotics in various types of complex water bodies was investigated, with digestate dewatering filtrate as a typical example. The establishment of this method provides a technical basis for further research on the detection of trace antibiotics in food waste digestate, and is also of great significance for the resource utilization of digestate filtrate.

Digestate filtrate samples used in this study were obtained from a food waste treatment plant and the Shanghai Municipal Bureau of the Environment (SMBE) in Jiading District, Shanghai, China. These were named Digestate filtrate1 and Digestate filtrate 2. Whose physicochemical properties of digestate filtrate 2 are shown in Table S1. Citric acid (CA)、and ammonium dihydrogen phosphate (NH4H2PO4) were purchased from Titan Technology Exploration Platform. Quinine sulfate、Dialysis bags (MW cut-off3500)、HCl、98 % H2SO4、NaOH、phosphate buffer saline (PBS)、Ciprofloxacin(C17H18FN3O3),Norfloxacin(C16H18FN3O3),Ofloxacin(C18H20FN3O4),Sulfamethoxazole(C10H11N3O3S),Roxithromycin(C41H76N2O15),and metal ion-containing (Ca2+、Al3+、Fe3+、Si4+、Cr6+、Na2+、Fe2+、Mg2+、Mn4+、Zn2+) solutions were purchased from Shanghai Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. All the chemicals were analytically pure.

UV/Vis absorption was measured using a UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan), fluorescence spectra were determined using a Dutta fluorescence and absorption spectrometer (Horiba, Kyoto, Japan), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed using a Thermo Scientific K-alpha (Thermo Fisher, Waltham, MA, USA), X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were performed using a Rigaku SmartLab SE (Japan), and Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) recordings were made using a Thermo Scientific Nicolette SE (Japan). USA) for X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements, Rigaku SmartLab SE (Japan) for X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements, Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) recordings were made using Thermo Scientific Scientific Nicolet iS20 (USA) to characterize the molecular structure, and Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) measurements were made using TESCAN MIRA LMS, Czech Republic, Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) measurements were carried out using a JEOL JEM-F200 (Japan), and the zeta potential was tested by ZETA SIZER Nano series instrumentation.

Citric Acid (4 g) and ammonium dihydrogen phosphate (0.5 g) were typically added to 40 ml of water to form a transparent homogeneous solution under ultrasonication treatment. The hydrothermal reaction was carried out at 200℃ for 6 hours to obtain a light- yellow mixture. Following the reaction, the resultant mixture was subsequently filtered through a 0.22μm filter membrane to eliminate any residual materials. The resultant yellowish aqueous dispersion of NP@CQDs was purified by dialysis using a cellulose membrane (3500 Da molecular weight cut-off) and ultra-pure water for 24 hours. The purified NP@CQDs solution was stored at 4℃ under light-proof conditions.

Synthesis of CQDs: 1 g citric acid was combined with 10 ml deionized water and sonicated to ensure homogeneity. The mixture was then transferred to a polytetrafluoroethylene reactor and hydrothermally heated at 200°C for 6 hours in a hydrothermal reaction unit. The resulting product was filtered through a 0.22μm membrane, and the solution obtained was dialyzed overnight using a 3500 Da dialysis bag. This process yielded a solution of CQDs, which was subsequently named CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence Quantum Yield and Relative Fluorescence Intensity
ce:section.ce:para:
The QY assay used in this study was calculated according to Eq. (1) with reference to [29] et al. With quinine sulfate (dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4, QY = 54 %):

Where; the subscripts 1 and 2 represent the unknown to be measured and the reference standard error respectively, QY is the fluorescence quantum yield, S is the fluorescence peak area (at the same excitation wavelength), A is the absorbance at 360 nm, and n refers to the refractive index of the solvent (the measurement of the concentration of the unknowns and the reference substance are extremely low in this experiment, so both are given a value of 1.33).

The relative fluorescence intensity F/F0 was calculated from the value of fluorescence intensity at its peak, according to Eq. (2):

Where; Y is the relative fluorescence intensity; F is the fluorescence intensity of 5 ml of NP@CQDs mixed with 5 ml of deionized water; and F0 is the fluorescence intensity of NP@CQDs with different concentrations (5 μM∼500 μM) of antibiotics (CIP, NOR, and OFLX) added.

For the stability study of NP@CQDs, the revision in fluorescence intensity was recorded at 360 nm in the pH range of 6.0–9.0, and the stability of NP@CQDs was measured by mixing them with NaCl solutions of different ionic strengths (0.5–3 mol/L) and various temperatures (20–80℃).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of NP@CQDs for quinolone antibiotics
Selectivity experiments for the detection of CIP, NOR, and OLFX by NP@CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: 5 ml of NP@CQDs and 5 ml of deionized water were mixed in a 10 ml cuvette and ultrasonically mixed for 15 min, and the fluorescence spectra were measured with 360 nm as the excitation wavelength λex, which is the initial fluorescence intensity of the NP@CQDs, F0. Then the deionized water was replaced into different metal ions (Ca2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Si4+, Cr6+, Na2+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Mn4+, Zn2+) and antibiotic (Sulfamethoxazole and Roxithromycin) with the concentration of 100μmol/L, and their fluorescence intensities were recorded as F at λex= 360 nm, and the influences of different interfering substances on fluorescence characteristics of NP@CQDs were investigated by the revision of F.

ce:section.ce:section-title: NP@CQDs detection of CIP, NOR, and OLFX in anti-interference experiments
ce:section.ce:para: 5 ml of 100μmol/L CIP, NOR, and OLFX solutions were mixed with 5 ml of NP@CQDs and other different interfering substances (Ca2+, Al3+, Fe3+, Si4+, Cr6+, Na2+, Fe2+, Mg2+, Mn4+, Zn2+, Sulphathiazole and Roxithromycin) in 15 ml of colorimetric tubes, respectively, and ultrasonic mixing was carried out for 15 min, with a fixed λex = 360 nm, and the fluorescence intensities, F, were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity experiments of NP@CQDs for the detection of CIP, NOR, OLFX
ce:section.ce:para: The quinolone antibiotics used in this study were CIP, NOR, and OFLX. Taking CIP as an example, the typical assay method was as follows: CIP solution (5 ml) with different concentrations (5–500μmol/L) was added to 5 ml of NP@CQDs, mixed for 10 min, and the fluorescence spectra and fluorescence intensities were measured and recorded. The concentrations of CIP and NOR were all in the range of 5–500 μM, and the OFLX concentrations ranged from 5 to 300 μM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of actual samples for the detection of three quinolone antibiotics
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the application of the probe in real water samples, Water samples were collected from four locations, namely, the lower reaches of the Fuxing Island Canal, the Yangtze River, Xuliujing Sampling Outlet, the digestate filtrate from the Jiading Wastewater Treatment Plant, and a sample of the Environment Bureau's digestate sludge. These water samples were filtered through a 0.45μm hydrophilic membrane to remove large particles from the water, and recovery experiments were performed. The standard concentrations added to the actual samples were 100μmol/L and 200μmol/L and the water samples were detected by NP@CQDs and the fluorescence spectra were recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural analysis of CQDs and NP@CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1(a), the CQDs was spherical or spheroidal with smooth surfaces and good dispersion. However, for the NP@CQDs, the surface morphology was revised, forming a predominantly massive structure, which was because p is doped into the structure[32]. The micromorphology of the NP@CQDs was evaluated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Fig. 1(b) shows that the CQDs were uniformly dispersed at lower resolutions and had a 0.224 nm lattice structure at higher resolutions, which corresponded to the graphene (100) plane[33]. Fig. 1(c) and(d) show the particle size distributions of CQDs and NP@CQDs. The calculated D50 of CQDs is 39.79 ± 4.88 nm, while the D50 of NP@CQDs is 175.00 ± 10.2 nm.

The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the CQDs and NP@CQDs further demonstrated variations in the samples' components and surface functional groups. Fig. 1(e) shows typical FTIR spectra of the hydrothermally synthesized CQDs and NP@CQDs, where the peak located at 3430 cm−1 is due to N-H stretching vibrations[34], and the peak at 2996 cm−1 can be attributed to the vibrational peaks of C-N or -OH. The peak near 1700 cm−1 was attributed to the asymmetry of the carboxyl CO functional group and the vibration of symmetric bonds[35], and the characteristic band at 1416 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibrations of -COOH and N-H [36]. Relative to that of the CQDs, the transmittance of the NP@CQDs exhibited a decreasing trend in these bands. In addition, the peaks in the NP@CQDs located at 1173 cm−1 and 939 cm−1 may be due to the P-O-R (R=alkyl) and P = O bonds[37], indicating that P was successfully doped into the CQDs. In addition, the peaks at 1047 cm−1 may be caused by C-N/N-O stretching vibration[38]. These results indicated the presence of carboxyl, amino, and hydroxyl groups on the surface of the NP@CQDs. Thus, NP@CQDs are aromatic nitrogen-phosphorus-doped carbon frameworks with many polar functional groups, which improve aqueous solubility and stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical phase analysis of CQDs and NP@CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was employed to validate the above findings and obtain more information on the revisions in the surface components of the carbon quantum dots before and after NP doping. As shown in Fig. 2(a), the NP@CQDs exhibited six characteristic peaks at 284.5 eV, 398.9 eV, 532.5 eV, 192.08 eV, 133.95 eV, and 26.08 eV, which correspond to C1s, N1s, O1s, P2s, P2p and N2s peaks, respectively. The carbonaceous structure of the material is confirmed. The calculated percentages of the four elements in the CQDs and NP@CQDs are presented in Table 1. All the above findings indicate that NP@CQDs are characterized by nitrogen and phosphorus enrichment. The high-resolution C1s spectrum of the NP@CQDs (Fig. 2(b)) has three peaks at 284.80, 286.92 and 288.95 eV, with the peak centered at 284.80 eV assigned to the (CC/C-C) in the graphene network associated with sp2 [39], corresponding to the graphitic carbon (002) diffraction peak in Fig.S1. The peak centered at 286.92 eV is attributed to sp3(C-C, C-N, C-O)[40]. This phenomenon suggests that the NP@CQDs are mainly carbon nano-network structures dominated by sp2 hybridization, and indirectly proves that the NP@CQDs have a higher electron cloud density. The peak at 288.95 eV represents the CN/CO bond, and the high oxidation state of carbon in NP@CQDs is demonstrated. A similar situation was observed in the high-resolution C1s spectra of the CQDs, as shown in Fig. S1, with three peaks at 284.77, 286.87, and 289.05 eV, indicating the presence of C-C, C-O, and CO bonds on the surface of the CQDs. Thus, we can conclude that the conjugated CC double bond is newly generated after NP doping, and this behavior promotes an increase in the conjugation of the NP@CQDs conjugated system. The high-resolution O1s map of the NP@CQDs is shown in Fig. 2(d) with two main bands at 532.65 and 533.68, corresponding to the C-O/P-O and CO bonds[41], respectively. This echoes the C1s spectra of the NP@CQDs shown in Fig. 2(b). The presence of the P-O bond proves the successful doping of p, as seen in the O1s spectrum of the CQDs. The high oxidation state of C was further demonstrated by comparing the vibrational amplitude of the two materials around 533.0 eV. The relatively subtle signals from the N1s and P2p high-resolution XPS spectra of the NP@CQDs suggest that the NP@CQDs were doped at a lower concentration, which is further supported by the comparison of the percentages of C, N, O, and P in the carbon quantum dots before and after doping (Table 1). The high-resolution spectrum of N1s (Fig. 2(c)) exhibited three peaks at 399.80, 400.60, and 401.48 eV, suggesting the presence of doped N in the form of graphitic N, pyridinic N and pyrrole N. These peaks correspond to the C-N, N-(C)3, and N-H bonds[37], respectively, with pyrrole N being the predominant form of nitrogen in the NP@CQDs. This suggests that nitrogen increases the photoexcited electrons in the emission-leap channel mainly through edge doping, which enhances the radiative-leap probability and PL intensity[42]. The high-resolution P2p spectrum is shown in Fig. 2(e), where the peak at 131.14 eV represents the presence of the P = O bond, and the two peaks at 133.12 and 133.86 eV are attributed to the P-O bond[43]. The presence of both proved the high oxidation state of P. The two peaks at 134.59 and 134.03 eV represent the presence of P-C bonds[43]. This study revealed that most doped nitrogen was bound to the CDs via N-H bonds, and P was bound via direct P-C bonds. The FTIR and XPS spectra prove that the surface of the NP-doped CQDs is rich in oxygen-containing, carboxyl, and amino functional groups.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) can be used to measure the crystallinity of nanomaterials, as shown in Fig.S1, where the CQDs show a broader diffraction peak around 2θ = 22°, which corresponds to the graphitic carbon (002) surface of the diffraction [33]. The broad diffraction peak of NP@CQDs, which was observed at 2θ ≈ 28.3°in Fig.S1, appeared to be shifted to a higher angle than that of CQDs because p was doped into the structure[32].
(3) 2dsinθ =nλ
Where, d represents the interlayer spacing. θ is the angle between the incident x-rays and the corresponding crystal plane. λ is the wavelength of the x-rays. In this experiment, Cu target Ka-rays were used, so λ = 0.15406 nm. n is the number of diffraction levels and n = 1.

According to Bragg's law(Eq. 3),the interlayer spacing of the CQDs plane was 0.4037 nm, while that of the NP@CQDs 002 plane and 100 plane was 0.3151 nm and 0.2130 nm, which were in agreement with the previous study[32], the offset of the peak to a higher angle (2θ) indicates a decrease in spacing. This may be due to the higher level of intermolecular attraction caused by intermolecular hydrogen bonding between the functional groups on the surface of the NP@CQDs[44]. This is attributed to the highly disordered carbon structure doped with N and P atoms[45], corresponding to an amorphous carbon structure.

As shown in Fig. 3(a), the NP@CQDs exhibit a photoexcitation-dependent property, red-shifting with increasing excitation wavelength. This finding is consistent with those of the previous studies[46]. An excitation wavelength of 360 nm was selected for subsequent experiments. Current research on the mechanism of photoexcitation dependence has not explicitly been elucidated, and previous studies[47] have shown that the photoluminescence behavior of carbon quantum dots is due to the synergistic effect of the energy wells on the surface and particle size. The quantum confinement effect indicates that the energy gap of the carbon quantum dots increases as the particle size decreases. Moreover, at the same time, the characterization of the NP@CQDs reveals that the presence of a variety of functional groups on the surface can further lead to a series of emission sites, the different surface states of the emission sites will vary with the excitation wavelength, thus resulting in excitation-dependent behaviors[48,49]. As shown in Fig. 3(b), Under UV irradiation, NP@CQDs showed a sharp absorption peak at 335 nm, which is attributed to the n-π* leaps of CO in the carbon dots[36] because both N and P co-doping provide additional electrons to the carbon dots, which is favorable for n-π* leaps[50], the results of FTIR analyses echo this. Additionally, as shown in Fig. 3(c), the fluorescence intensity of the NP@CQDs was much higher than that of CQDs. In addition, the emission wavelength had a wider range. The relative photoluminescence QY of the prepared NP@CQDs at 360 nm was 43 %, using quinine sulfate as a reference (see Table S2 for details). Its QY was also 260.7 % higher than the CQDs (11.92 %).

On the one hand, a part of the nitrogen is well doped into the carbon network structure in the form of graphitic nitrogen, which introduces other off-domain π-electrons, increases the degree of π-electron conjugation, increases the radiative excursion probability and the photoluminescence intensity[42], and triggers the phenomenon of redshift of the emission wavelength. These results correspond to the high-resolution N1s mapping and fluorescence spectra of the NP@CQDs (Fig. 3(a)). In contrast doping with p triggers the oxidation of the surface state, and the onset of the oxidized state introduces defects, which can act as trapping centers for excitons, thus triggering fluorescence emission behaviors associated with the surface state and increasing the probability of radiative excursion and photoluminescence intensity[51]. High-resolution XPS maps of P2p indicate a high oxidation/defect density on the surface of the NP@CQDs[37]. In addition, the presence of an amino group as an electron-donating group on the surface of the NP@CQDs increases electron could density and improves the degree of conjugation of the conjugated system, increases the jump from the ground state to the lowest excited single-wire state, and improves the fluorescence QY[52,53].

As shown in Fig. 3(d), the relative photoluminescence intensity of the NP@CQDs showed an overall smooth state within six hours, although there was a range fluctuation, which fully demonstrated that the prepared composites had better fluorescence stability within a short period. Because the conditions of antibiotic presence in the actual environment or biological samples are very complex, there are more influencing factors. Therefore, in this study, NP@CQDs were subjected to different pH and ionic strength conditions to evaluate their effects on luminescence intensity. As shown in Fig. 3(e), the fluorescence intensity of the NP@CQDs fluctuated with an increase in pH in the range of 6–9 (with HCl and NaOH as the controlling pH factors). Under strong acid and alkali conditions, the morphology and number of defects on the surface of carbon quantum dots are altered, and these defects act as trapping centers for electrons or holes, affecting the electron-leaping process of the carbon quantum dots and leading to a consequent variation in fluorescence intensity[36]. The pH of the digestate filtrate used in this study was near 7.5, so a pH of 7.5 was used in the subsequent experiments for detecting CIP, NOR, and OFX. Fig. 3(f) shows the variations in the fluorescence intensity of the NP@CQDs at NaCl concentrations ranging from 0.5μmol/L to 3.0μmol/L. Typically, Na+, as an alkali metal ion, interacts with the functional groups on the surface of the carbon quantum dots to form ligand bonds, etc., resulting in an increase in the electron-hole pair complexing efficiency, leading to revisions in fluorescence intensity[54]. In this study, the fluorescence intensity was very stable because of the lack of ionization of the groups on the surface of the NP@CQDs. Finally, the effect of temperature on the fluorescence intensity of NP@CQDs was discussed. As shown in the Fig. 3(g), the increase of temperature leads to the continuous quenching of fluorescence intensity. This is consistent with previous studies[55]. The effect of temperature on carbon quantum dots is mainly reflected in the intensification of the thermal movement of molecules leading to carbon quantum dots collision burst phenomenon. Carbon quantum dots in the excited state of the electrons have not yet emitted fluorescence by means of radiation, due to the thermal movement of the surrounding molecules collision or in the form of heat and other forms of release, resulting in a fluorescence quenching[56]. Ultimately, the remarkable stability of NP@CQDs enables them to detect target products without interference from complex environmental factors (Placement time, pH, NaCl concentration, etc.).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of CIP, NOR and OFLX
Analytical selectivity for three quinolone antibiotics
ce:section.ce:para: The results shown in Fig. 4 were used to assess the analytical selectivity of the prepared NP@CQDs for three quinolone antibiotics (CIP, NOR, and OFLX). It can be seen that most of the metals have a quenching effect on the fluorescence intensity of NP@CQDs, and at the same time, the effect of two other kinds of antibiotics on their fluorescence appears to be similar. Only with the addition of CIP, NOR, and OFLX, there was very good sensitization of the fluorescence of NP@CQDs, which was approximately 754 %, 125 %, and 80 %, respectively. These results prove that NP@CQDs have a strong selectivity effect on CIP, NOR, and OFLX. The NP@CQDs composite was rich in carboxyl and hydroxyl groups, forming strong hydrogen bonds with quinolone antibiotics and enhancing emission intensity[57].

The test results are shown in Fig.S3. After the addition of interfering substances (e.g., Ca2+、Al3+、Fe3+) at a concentration of 100μmol/L, the test substance can still produce a strong enhancement effect on the fluorescence intensity of NP@CQDs, which can prove that the detection of CIP, NOR and OFLX by NP@CQDs is not interfered by the presence of other species, and shows the excellent anti-interference performance of NP@CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of a method for determination of quinolone antibiotics in food waste digestate filtrate
ce:section.ce:para: As depicted in Fig. 5(a), the fluorescence intensity of NP@CQDs rose with the elevation of CIP concentration, while the position of the fluorescence emission peaks remained stable. Upon reaching a concentration of 100μmol/L, the fluorescence sensitization efficiency reached 86.9 %; when the concentration reached 200μmol/L, the efficiency surged to 237.7 %.

As shown in Fig. 5(b), the relative fluorescence intensity F/F0 curve was fitted, and a good linear relationship between its enhancement efficiency and CIP concentration was found. Its F/F0 curve was corrected to be F/F0= 0.017X+ 0.11 and its R 2= 0.993 for concentrations from 5μmol/L to 200μmol/L, and the same phenomenon occurred between 250μmol/L and 500μmol/L, and its curve was F/F0= 0.0078X+ 1.5867, its R 2= 0.993, and after calculation limit of detection (LOD)= 0.43μmol/L.

Both NOR and OFLX showed a similar phenomenon, as can be seen in Fig. 5, a good linear relationship between NOR concentration and fluorescence enhancement efficiency was observed at 5–100μmol/L versus 120–500μmol/L, with the curves of F/F0= 0.0458X+ 0.4905 (R 2=0.994) and F/F0= 0.0227X+ 3.8742 (R 2=0.991). The detection limit was 0.075μmol/L. The linearity of OFLX concentration was better at 5 ∼300μmol/L with the curve of F/F0= 0.047X+ 0.6656 (R 2=0.991). The LOD was 0.035μmol/L. Their linear curves all showed some deviation from the linear upward bending, which proved the existence of a self-absorption or energy-transfer process[58]. Compared with the reported methods (Table S3), this new method has a lower detection limit and a wider detection range. It has a promising application in the detection of quinolone antibiotics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection applications in water samples and digestate filtrate
ce:section.ce:para: Two water samples and two digestate filtrate samples were subjected to the application of antibiotics according to the steps described in the appeal section to confirm that these NP@CQDs can be applied to detect the three quinolone antibiotics in real samples. The results were all detected, so antibiotic solutions with concentrations of 100μmol/L and 200μmol/L were added to the filtrate water samples for spiking recovery experiments, and the tests were conducted three times in parallel, in which the results of CIP are shown in Table S4. The recoveries of CIP spiked in the actual water samples ranged from 93.73 % to 109.22 %, and the relative standard deviations were all less than 4.48 %. (The NOR and OFLX results are presented in Table S5 and S6). The results presented above indicate that the three oxyquinoline antibiotic detection methods developed in this study, which are based on the fluorescence sensitization effect of NP@CQDs, demonstrate high accuracy and reproducibility in both simple aqueous and complex organic waste environments. Moreover, this study is the inaugural application of a carbon quantum dot-based fluorescent probe for the detection of antibiotics in food waste digestate filtrate, as evidenced by a comparison with previous studies (Table S7). This study innovatively applied carbon quantum dot-based fluorescent probes to the detection of antibiotics in the digestate filtrate of food waste in a complex water environment for the first time. This breakthrough finding has played an extremely crucial foundational role in the subsequent development of detection methods and the expansion of related applications centered around carbon quantum dots. Furthermore, this finding is of paramount importance for the subsequent effective utilization of food waste digestate filtrate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of fluorescence sensitization of quinolone antibiotics by NP@CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 6(a), the core structure of quinolone antibiotics[59] can be identified as an oxyquinoline when the X position is carbon, based on the element at this position. There are two major groups in it, namely groups of quinolones. If the X position is nitrogen, it is defined as a naphthyridine antibiotic. As shown in Fig. 6(b), the three antibiotics examined in this study belong to the oxyquinoline group.

To further explore the interaction mechanism between NP@CQDs and antibiotics, we selected typical organic compounds corresponding to the typical functional groups on the surface of antibiotics and conducted binding experiments by combining them with NP@CQDs respectively. As shown in Fig. 6(c), quinolone antibiotics contain groups such as -F, aromatic hydrocarbons, piperazinyl rings, and carbonyls. Therefore, compounds containing the same groups as quinolone antibiotics were selected and tested (for example, PTFE, Toluene, 1.4-Piperazine ethylenediamine, and N-Methylpyrrolidone). As shown in Fig. 6(c), only when toluene and PTFE were added did the fluorescence intensity increase substantially. Based on this observation, it can be concluded that toluene and PTFE exert a fluorescence sensitization effect on the NP@CQDs. Furthermore, it can be inferred that the combination of the benzene ring and C-F bond with NP@CQDs is the main reason for the increase in fluorescence intensity. This is a fluorescence enhancement effect based on substituent effects. Specifically, in these antibiotics, the -F bond replaces the H bond in the benzene ring. This substitution leads to an increase in the density of the π electron cloud on the benzene ring and an enhancement in the degree of conjugation, which consequently results in enhanced fluorescence[60]. As demonstrated in Fig. 7(a), this substitution-induced fluorescence enhancement effect also implies that the antibiotic itself might have strong fluorescence characteristics.

Furthermore, Characterization experiments of NP@CQDs had demonstrated the presence of abundant amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups on their surfaces. The lone pair electrons on electron-donating groups were transferred to the ring to produce excited states. Since the electron cloud of these lone pair electrons was almost parallel to the π-orbitals on the aromatic ring[61], they in fact shared the conjugated π-electronic structure and, at the same time, expanded their conjugated double bond system[62]. This resulted in a fluorescence-sensitizing effect[60]. To further explore the fluorescence variation mechanism of NP@CQDs, cyclic voltammetry (CV) was employed. As shown in Fig. S4, the onset of the reduction and oxidation potentials for NP@CQDs, denoted as Ered and Eox respectively, were 0.47 eV and 0.58 eV respectively. Furthermore, the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) and Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO) values for NP@CQDs were calculated as −4.98 eV and −4.87 eV respectively, based on the empirical equations represented by Eq. 4 and Eq. 5[29]. The results indicated that the LUMO of NP@CQDs was lower than that of antibiotics (-4.7 eV)[63]. This made it difficult for electrons to be transferred from the excited state of NP@CQDs to the acceptor part of the antibiotics anymore, and thus the photoinduced electron transfer (PET) process was inhibited[64]. As a result, the light-excited electrons in NP@CQDs could then usually jump back to the original ground-state orbitals, thereby enhancing the fluorescence signal of NP@CQDs (Fig. 6(d)).
(4)

In this study, the UV absorption of the quinolone antibiotics was tested, and the results were shown in Fig. 7(b). There was no significant overlap between the UV–vis absorption spectra of CIP, NOR, and OFLX and the fluorescence spectra of the NP@CQDs, thus ruling out the presence of fluorescence energy resonance transfer (FRET) [65] and the internal filtering effect (IFE) [66]. Based on the previous analysis and to further understand the interaction mechanism between the antibiotics and NP@CQDs, we investigated the surface charges of them. As illustrated above, the presence of many electron-donating groups on the surfaces of the antibiotics and NP@CQDs also contributed to the possibility that the surfaces of the antibiotics and NP@CQDs might carry a negative charge. After measurement, the results were shown in Table S8. The surface charge of NP@CQDs was determined to be −6.318 ± 1.62 mV, which was consistent with previous studies[67], while the surface charges of CIP, NOR, and OFLX were −1.74 ± 0.67 mV, −1.93 ± 0.19 mV, and −3.05 ± 1.87 mV, respectively. This led to electrostatic repulsion between the antibiotic and the carbon quantum dots, and the repulsive force acted as an energy source for electron jumps, increasing their frequency and indirectly leading to enhanced fluorescence.

The presence of graphite nitrogen, pyridinic nitrogen and pyrrole nitrogen groups in NP@CQDs increases the π-electron cloud density compared to CQDs, leading to the enhancement of the conjugation degree of the conjugated system. In addition, the probability of radiative transition and photoluminescence intensity are increased, and the emission wavelength redshift phenomenon is triggered. Introducing phosphorus triggers oxidation of the carbon dot surface states, introducing more surface defects, increasing electron leaps from the ground state to the lowest excited single-line state, and improves the fluorescence quantum yield.

A positive linear correlation was observed between the fluorescence intensity of NP@CQDs and the antibiotic concentration. The phase correlation curves for CIP, NOR, and OFLX were found to follow the equations: F/F0 = 0.0078X + 1.5867 (R
2 = 0.993), F/F0 = 0.0458X + 0.4902 (R
2 = 0.994), and F/F0 = 0.047X + 0.6656 (R
2 = 0.991). The limit of detection was as low as 0.42 μmol/L, 0.075 μmol/L, and 0.043 μmol/L. Furthermore, the high quantum yield NP@CQDs were successfully employed as fluorescent probes for the determination of the three oxyquinoline antibiotics in the filtrate of food waste digestate, and all demonstrated satisfactory spiked recoveries and low relative standard deviations (＜10 %).

The benzene ring structure and C-F bonds in quinolone antibiotics combined with NP@CQDs were the main reasons for the fluorescence enhancement for detecting antibiotics. Many electron-donating groups existed on the surfaces of the antibiotics and NP@CQDs, leading to a negative surface charge and facilitating the substituent effect. The electron-donating groups on the surface of NP@CQDs bind to the aromatic ring structure of the antibiotic, thereby extending the conjugated double bond system and inhibiting PET, allowing fluorescence recovery. At the same time, electrostatic repulsion between the NP@CQDs and the antibiotics will indirectly provide energy support for the electron leaps and further enhance the radiative leap probability and fluorescence intensity.
